author: @Ace-rr7ww
text:
Venant d’un Algérien aveuglé par la haine que lui inculquent leurs médias poubelles 24h/24 contre le Maroc, rien de surprenant. Ce sont les mêmes médias et les mêmes pseudo-consultants qui affirmaient que, si leur équipe perdait contre la Jordanie, ils devraient arrêter le football.

Face au Nigeria, cette équipe a été inexistante : ce n’était même pas un match, on aurait dit une équipe senior jouant contre des enfants. Pourtant, malgré cela, ils continuent d’attaquer les autres uniquement pour exister à travers leurs médias toxiques et leur propagande permanente.

Et juste pour rappeler un fait que certains préfèrent ignorer : le Maroc a bel et bien battu le Brésil en match amical il y a deux ou trois ans. Mais quand on est aveuglé par la haine on devient incapable de reconnaître la réalité.

author: @Simonserfati
text:
L'affaire Daniel Galvan Viña (2013) — "le DanielGate"
Ce ressortissant espagnol avait été condamné au Maroc à 30 ans de prison pour le viol de 11 enfants marocains. Il fut gracié par le roi Mohammed VI.
L'affaire Calvez (2013) — réseau pédophile à Casablanca
Un journaliste français d'une soixantaine d'années, Calvez Yvon Guillaume Albert, dirigeait depuis sa résidence à Casablanca un réseau pédophile avec l'aide de deux femmes de ménage marocaines qui lui fournissaient régulièrement des fillettes âgées d'une dizaine d'années. Certains viols étaient filmés. 
L'affaire Soumère (2006) — Marrakech
Jacques-Henri Soumère, patron de l'opéra de Massy et ex-directeur du théâtre Mogador à Paris, avait été surpris en flagrant délit de pédophilie avec deux jeunes garçons mineurs à Marrakech. Des cassettes pornographiques avaient été saisies. Malgré le flagrant délit, il n'écopa que de quatre mois de prison avec sursis et 500 dirhams d'amende, avant de repartir librement en France.  
L'affaire Philippe Servaty — "le pornographe d'Agadir"
Cet ex-journaliste belge venait fréquemment au Maroc entre 2001 et 2004. À Agadir, il abordait des adolescentes issues de milieux très pauvres, leur promettait une vie meilleure, et les convainquait de se laisser photographier nues dans des positions dégradantes avant de diffuser les images sur internet
L'affaire de l'Espagnol à Errachidia
Un ressortissant espagnol de 38 ans avait loué une maison à Errachidia dans le seul but d'accueillir des mineurs pour les exploiter sexuellement. Il se faisait passer pour le président d'une association de bienfaisance et promettait de faciliter l'émigration en Espagne.  
L'affaire de Jacques Leveugle — Khénifra
Pendant des décennies, ce discret professeur français, investi dans la vie locale à Khénifra, a abusé de nombreux mineurs. C'est en France, et non au Maroc, que le scandale a éclaté : il fut mis en examen pour viols et agressions sexuelles sur 89 garçons âgés de 13 à 17 ans, commis sur plus de cinquante ans dans plusieurs pays, sans qu'aucune procédure judiciaire n'ait été engagée contre lui au Maroc. 
L'affaire des Koweïtiens à Marrakech (2024)
Sept étrangers de nationalité koweïtienne, impliqués dans une affaire de tourisme sexuel à Marrakech, ont pu fuir le Maroc avant même leur procès,
Un problème structurel
Le Maroc est décrit par les organisations de défense des droits de l'enfant comme une destination de plus en plus prisée des pédophiles, en raison de sa proximité avec l'Europe, de la pauvreté de nombreux enfants, et de la faiblesse des structures juridiques. De manière paradoxale, les enfants exploités sont souvent traités comme des délinquants plutôt que des victimes par le code pénal marocain. 
Un schéma récurrent est également dénoncé : les ressortissants étrangers pris en flagrant délit bénéficient de libertés provisoires contre caution et quittent impunément le territoire, tandis que les jeunes filles marocaines impliquées sont, elles, incarcérées.

author: @frbillnich
text:
Just recently came across this video.  So I am a bit of a "come lately" here.  However, "Casablanca" is my all time favorite movie.  So I want to add a comment.

For me, one of the best "actions-speak-louder-than-words" moments of character development is at the conclusion of the "Knock on Wood" musical number (during which Rick hides the letters in Sam's piano, Ferrari enters the Cafe and makes knowing (to Rick's stoic) eye contact with Rick).  

At the conclusion of the song, as the audience applauds, Sam smiles to the audience, stands and bows . . .  to RICK.

A great gesture that indicates that Sam is more than just an employee.  Later you get to see he is also a close friend, who shares a percentage in the Cafe's profits.  Maybe, like Rick, perhaps with Rick, he also has a past as a freedom fighter.

author: @solracer66
text: The point that Rick rejoins the fight in the film lasts all of a second or two, yet without it the film would have been nowhere near as powerful. The shot happens right after Victor Lazlo commands the band to play La Marseillaise and the band members quickly glance at Rick who then nods his permission. That made the scene and the movie much more powerful than if the band had followed Lazlo's instructions without asking Rick whether it was ok or not. This shows that even a quick shot that's gone in an instant can be as important to the story as any other longer exposition.

author: @rumi9005
text:
The 'big macguffin' in Casablanca, the letters of transit, are in the best tradition of macguffins as defined by Hitchcock himself.
Because the letters are not a real thing. There never was anything like a 'letter of transit' issued by any side or authority during the Second World War. At least, not the bearer bond, blank cheque, 'get out of jail free' kind like those in the movie.

author: @hebneh
text: It's a miracle that "Casablanca" turned out to be a masterpiece considering the entire scriptwriting process was a disorganized mess, with rewrites being given to the performers almost daily and a planned additional finale in which Rick and Capt. author: @davidmoser8845
text: One interesting tidbit in the script that I didn’t notice until after many viewings: when Victor asks Rick why he won’t give him the letters of transit, Rick tells him to “ask his wife.” Although Victor does not react visibly, this must have been a bit of a shock to him, as their marriage was a very closely held secret. Rick understood that, so it comes off as a bit of a power play - “I know more about what’s going on than you think I do.” The point is reinforced later when Victor relates the story to Ilsa. He doesn’t just say that Rick told him to ask her; he pointedly clarifies, “He told me to ask… my wife.” It’s Victor’s own little power play, showing that he knows that only Ilsa could have revealed their secret, and thus her relationship with Rick must be very intimate and trusting, more than she had let on.

It was he who wrote the love theme which compelled the entire script.  He set down all the elements driving Rick, Ilsa, and Victor, and wove them into this captivating story.  He got not one word of credit onscreen, and when the Epstein boys and Koch picked up their Oscars, he said no, he didn't feel cheated--that when he wins for screenwriting he doesn't want other guys' names on that Oscar with his.

author: @constancewalsh3646
text:
For the umnpteenth time, I weep from start to finish. Why? not because of war, or the sadness of sacrifice and separation (although this too). 
It's the genius of this film that brings tears, the intelligence, the beauty, the humor, gestures of boldness and courage, of respect, of love. 
The subtleties of friendship and shared values, in action.  
That so many contributed to the integrity of this oeuvre in all aspects, that their personal talents were so amazing - this is what moves me so deeply.
It is what I miss in the world, in art which has become excessively individualized and competitive, though not everywhere.  
Thank you for this wonderful analysis and appreciation of one of the greatest American movies of all time, if not the greatest!

author: @malimal9191
text:
‘Casablanca’ is renowned and is justly acknowledged as one of the most romantic films of all time but it is much more than the tale of a love  triangle. Of course, it shows that the power of love can affect  the human psyche, as demonstrated by Rick’s metamorphosis, but, actually, the film is one of the most subtle pieces of propaganda ever made.

Made in the bleakest times of WW2, this film has so many levels to it that it takes many viewings to appreciate them. The main theme is not romance but self-sacrifice as its message to the world at war is to give up the personal agenda for the common cause. It reminds wartime audiences, many of whom have loved ones fighting abroad, that their situation is the same as that of Rick, Ilsa and Victor.

Rick’s initial selfishness, (‘I stick my neck out for nobody’ and ‘the problems of the world are not in my department…’), is a metaphor for USA indifference. It must be remembered that the events and politics are hard to comprehend and put into perspective for current audiences than for those living through WW2, not knowing who the victors would be.

The script can be considered as a 'State of the Union' address, both for home and foreign policies, in which there are references to Civil Rights, as embodied in Sam and, of course, the debate about America’s involvement in the conflict. Basically, the film is politically motivated because it is a plea to America to join the war. Please note that the action takes place in pre-Pearl Harbour, December, 1941.

The screenplay is so intelligently written. It is a masterpiece of complexity, containing subliminal political opinions and messages all carried along on a thrilling plot with brilliant one-liners and memorable quotes, together with comedic elements and contemporary, social commentaries. 

Michael Curtiz’s direction is multi-faceted: Documentary, Film Noir, German Expressionism, Flashback etc. He is the master of creating the plot via seamlessly connecting a series of rapid-fire vignettes.

There is subtle direction and cinematography. For example, Ilsa wears black and white clothes and is cast in shadows and in a mirror which symbolise the ambiguity of her role.


POINTS OF INTEREST AND NOTES FOR SUBSEQUENT VIEWINGS.

This is the the first non-musical movie to use music almost as an another protagonist, (which Tarantino does now). For example, ‘As Time Goes By’ is a valuable recurring theme and, in Paris, Rick and Ilsa dance to ‘Perfidia’ which means untrustworthiness.  Also, ‘Love for Sale’ is played during the dialogue when the Bulgarian girl tells Rick about her ‘offer’ from Renault.

Each character represents a country e.g. Two Japanese plotting; the Italian on the tail of the German; American indifference; French collaborators; the British robbed by foreign policy. Even the Balkan problem , (still ongoing), are mentioned via the Bulgarian couple. Quite evidently, Rick’s actions symbolise the USA in its change in policy from isolationism to participation and ‘….the beginning of a beautiful friendship…’ is the USA and Europe joining forces to fight Nazism. 

The significance of Letters of Transit is a metaphor for the might of America’s power and resources and must be delivered to the right side.

The ‘La Marseillaise’ scene is the pivotal moment in which both Ilsa and Rick realise that saving Victor is more important than their own personal relationship. It also comes in just as Rick and Victor are about to argue over Ilsa but both drop the issue when they hear the music. This scene is rousing now but imagine how it must have felt for audiences right in the middle of the war when Germany seemed invincible and modern viewers need to put it in perspective in terms of world events full of Nazi and Japanese domination and when the outcome looked very bleak. 

The facial close-ups used throughout the film speak a thousand words: but particularly note Ilsa during ‘La Marseillaise’ when her expressions show her admiration of Victor’s power and her realisation that this must be preserved at all costs.

POINTS TO WATCH

‘It’s December, 1941 in Casablanca: what time is it in New York?...
I bet they are asleep all over America’. PEARL HARBOUR

‘Even Nazis can’t kill that fast’
CONCENTRATION CAMPS

‘I don’t buy or sell human beings..’
CIVIL RIGHTS

In any case... there is so much alcohol!!!! On this note, please watch out for glasses knocked over and glasses set upright… 

The Bulgarian couple keep appearing  many times as symbols of hope and determination.

In the bar room fight over Yvonne, Rick attacks the German only and not the Frenchman.

Captain Renault dumps the bottle of Vichy water to represent his rejection of the Nazi- collaborating French Government which was located in Vichy. 

Just one example of the excellent and complex scriptwriting occurs immediately after the roulette scene. The girl thanks Rick for letting her husband win and Rick replies, ‘He’s just a lucky guy’, which, on the face of it, refers to the gambling, but, in Rick’s mind, means that the husband is ‘lucky’ because his partner truly loves him. 

Please imagine what hope the dialogue  must have projected when Ilsa states that she’ll wear the blue dress again when Paris is liberated. Nobody then knew when this would be.

The quotes from the film are now embedded in popular culture and are mostly said by Rick. However, Captain Renault has some of the best lines: e.g. when asking Rick why he had to leave America, he says, ‘I’d like to think you killed a man: it’s the romantic in me’ ; a gunshot to his heart would be his ‘..least vulnerable part..’; when told where the Letters of Transit were hidden in the piano, ‘’…it’s my fault for not being musical…’: on making the bet with Rick, …’make it 10,000 - I’m only a poor corrupt official…’

author: @cokonutraw8800
text:
What makes this movie & script brilliant, what makes it possibly the best is that… 

It is the birth of the scene. 

If you’ve seen this movie, you can recall so many scenes. This will become the standard for hollywood to follow forever more, there is a satisfaction in ‘the scene’, and You Tube is proof that people will continually flock to scenes. Hell, most people only can remember a movie by a scene. And, scenes span the gamut - the tarring the rooftop scene in Shawshank Redemption, the dance-off scene in American Wedding, the I am your Father scene from ESB, the Quarterback is toast scene from Die Hard, the Lois falling from a helicopter scene from Superman, the Daniel Laruso is going to fight scene from Karate Kid, the scene of Joker talking to the mob in Dark Knight, the Arnold covered in mud scene in Predator, the Don’t call me Shirley scene from Airplane, the Scotty Doesn’t Know scene from Euro Trip, the I wanna go home scene from Forrest Gump, the He jacked Roger scene from Training Day, the Do you think i’m a little mary ann scene from One Flew Over the Cuckoo’s nest, the shower scene from Psycho, the Stella scene from ASND, the leg lamp scene from Christmas Story, the bus scene from The Graduate, the first major battle scene in Braveheart, the ______ scene from Pride & Prejudice, and don’t get me started on The Godfather (which is nothing but one scene after another). 

But, that was the resonance of Casablanca, it’s several scenes which the audience took away with it.. a legacy which remains to this very day. What is behind the allure of a scene, why do they haunt us.. i dunno. But, I can’t think of a movie prior to Casablanca which exemplified the concept of a scene better.. so i credit it as the game changer.

author: @williamjones7163
text: 40 years ago I took a class at the local college called WWII Propaganda in Film. I choose to review Casablanca. I had never seen the film before this class. My review was boy meets girl, boy loses girl, boy gets girl back, but loses her to the campus hero. My professor commented "Well, that is one persons opinion." I did not know that this was his all time favorite movie and I had just dismissed it as a Beach Blanket Bingo with Annette and Frankie movie. Now I have matured and can understand the movie. The times it was made. The story of the events. The conflicts involved. After seeing so much schlock in the cinema, I can see why it was my professor's favorite film.

author: @LazlosPlane
text:
​@ScriptSleuth   ok.   that's a reasonable answer.   I wonder if you don't mind, while I have your attention if I may ask another question about something that has annoyed me for some time.....
]
At the airport, before he says his final farewells to Victor and Ilsa,  Rick confesses that Ilsa was at his place and that, "she did everything in her power to convince me that she was still in love with me, and for my sake I let her pretend...."  Whoa!  Rick is essentially telling Victor that he banged his wife the night before and Victor responds, "I understand..."  What???   

First, WHY does Rick even bother telling Lazlo that Ilsa was in his apartment the night before???    What's the point?  What difference will it make down the road...?    
Second,  why confess to having sex with her?
Third, why, instead of plugging him in the jaw, does Victor respond, "I understand." ?

THANKS!

author: @ScriptSleuth
text: Good question. I go back to the conversation Victor had with Ilsa on a previous night, when he asked her if she was lonely while he was in prison camp. He seemed to understand she had needs that he wasn't giving her. Their relationship seemed more platonic to me.

author: @LazlosPlane
text: @ScriptSleuth    Weak.  I don't see any evidence for that.   Plus, you are intuiting that Rick is intuiting that Ilsa needs sex.  Hmmm....  apparantly you only think of Victor as the leader of a cause but he is also 'A MAN."  (where'd I hear that?)  No there is no reason to think Ilsa isn't getting any.  It also doesn't explain why Rick tells Victor that they did the deed, so to speak.  Makes no sense.  Did he expect Victor to say, "Oh, thanks, pal!"   Then, when they get to LIsbon she's going to find someone else?  Hmmmm.....

author: @charlie-obrien
text:
In the scene just before, Rick very coldly refuses to sell the Letters of Transit to Lazlo, then the German music starts and Lazlo retaliates by leading the house band in playing “La Marseillaise” and the entire cafe joins in singing.
This is a pivotal moment when the entire movie changes direction. Rick approves the playing even though he knows it will cost him, the scenes change after that from mostly well lit daytime shots to dark and ominous night time and Claude Rains character becomes more aggressive towards Rick.
It's a fantastic scene and there are simply not many that compare.

author: @ScriptSleuth
text: Since the story would not make much sense if we didn't know the backstory of Rick and Ilsa, the flashback in this movie is quite crucial. It provides Rick's character ghost and why he acts the way he does. It's the key incident, without which the story would not exist.

author: @rizqbeckett7086
text: What’s amazing about this movie is the things that I notice almost every time I watch it. I noticed that Ilsa has never told her husband that she loves him, although he often declares his love to her and not only in words, but she does say it to Rick. But there’s no denying that she admires her husband and it’s apparent why, in spite of the threats from the Nazis his courage and commitment is outstanding. There’s an incident that causes Rick to switch his mind from his original plan to allow Victor to get away alone without his wife. I think it’s when Victor unselfishly asks Rick to use the letters of transits to take his wife away after telling Rick to his face that he knows that he loves his wife too. I also think that Rick hated when Louie compared his low immoral self to him. The key word was when he asked her to trust him. Because in the next scene when Louie tried to arrest Victor….well the rest of the movie is perhaps one of the greatest endings in movie history with four words before walking away with Louie, “Here’s looking at you kid!”.

author: @jackrice2770
text:
I see a few film students have been assigned this film. They should note a couple of important things: 

1) Note there isn't any 'filler', not one unnecessary scene. 

2) Instead of the modern film style with naked, sweaty bodies flopping around for several minutes, here we see symbols and subtle shots that tell us that yes, Ilsa and Rick "do it" but we're spared the pace-killing soft-core porn so prevalent today. (That shot of the searchlight when the camera cuts away). Yes, Bogie and Ilsa are wearing clothes in the next shot, but apparently folks in the '40s were a bit quicker on the uptake than today. 

3) The script is tight, and although at least three writers worked on it, the final result works perfectly. The sign of a director in complete control of his project.

4) The shadows and camera angles are works of genius. Lighting designers and cinematographers should watch it frame by frame.

author: @jackrice2770
text:
Yes, in case you're a young'un, this movie has been voted one of the best by any number of film associations, One historical note: Renault says to Strasser that "he was with the Americans when they blundered into Berlin in 1918." Germany was never invaded during the First World War, and certainly no foreign troops, American or otherwise, entered the capital. Of course, one shouldn't rely on films for historical facts (actually, almost all 'historical' films are grossly inaccurate).

Here's looking at you, Kid.

author: @timothydavis1885
text: There have been few movies that have equaled the romantic twists, the suspense, the drama, the musical soundtrack, as Casablanca.   As Time Goes By will go down in cinematic history as one of the greatest musical masterpieces of all time.   This was undoubtedly Humphrey Bogart's greatest work. Humphrey Bogart was unquestionably one of the greatest actors ever to grace movie screen.  Ingrid Bergman will forever be one of the most beautiful woman ever to cross Hollywood, whose theatrical performance in Casablanca seemed as though she were living out her own life, which just happened to be portrayed in this timeless work of cinematic art.   Paul Heinreid was the ultimate patriot, a part he played so well, it would go down as one of the most realistic performances ever.  This movie is without doubt one of the top 10 movies Hollywood ever produced.

text: Rick wasn't helping Victor and Ilsa until she explained her train station no-show. He finally got that, then had to get Victor out of Captain Reynaud's custody and set up the pair's clean getaway. Rick realized that him and Ilsa having the hots for each other paled beside the importance of Victor's mission, and that staying together in Casablanca was impossibly dangerous. The script writers made sure Rick didn't have any easy outs, just a tough one.

text: This one should never be remade.  No one could come close to duplicating the performances of Bogart, Bergman, Henried, or Rains.  These actors each had such a unique style------I don't believe that any combination of actors today could come together the way that they did.  The re-make of "Sebrina" fell flat.  Even with stars like Harrison Ford, Richard Kinnear, and Julia Ormond, the 1995 version does not compare to the 1954 original, starring Humphrey Bogart, William Holden, and Audrey Hepburn.  I fear the same would happen with an attempt to re-make "Casablanca".

author: @anglophils645
text: @tiffsaverThe only classic that I can think of, where the re-make is possibly as good or better than the original, is Pride and Prejudice.  The original starring Greer Garson and Lawrence Olivier, is not necessarily better than the 1995 version starring Colin Firth and Jennifer Ehle.  The 2005 version starring Keira Knightley and Donald Sutherland, among others, is also very good.

author: @tiffsaver
text:
@anglophils645 
Same thing with sequels.  But for my money, the best, and very first sequel of all time is "The Bride of Frankenstein," in 1935.

I'll never know, but I wonder sometimes whether Rains' character was so named because of Louis Renault and his company.
I'm not sure how widely Renault's passive (?) collaboration with the German occupiers was known in Hollywood at the time this film was written/made.
However... Captain Renault lives in Vichy Casablanca in the very same fog of deceit and pretence that Louis Renault used to profit from occupation. 
I may be wrong.
But I do wonder...

author: @Boug0014
text: It’s quite interesting with those street guides.  Some (I’m not saying all), don’t even know the history …. The Portuguese never occupied Rabat…. They occupied Maamoura in 1515 about 30km north of Rabat and Casablanca in also 1515.  But they never were in Rabat.  The Oudaya Kasbah was built by the Almohad Royal Family in 1150 as a fortification (Ribat) to fight the Berghwata Kingdom that extended from south of Rabat to about Essaouira.  The Kasbah was later used as a settlement for the Andalus Refugees after the fall of Granada and the expulsion of the Moriscos from Spain.

author: @J_Ges
text:
Hi Chris, compliments on your videos, I really enjoy them!
I’m a street photographer living in London and in March I’m going for a week in Marrakech. I’ve heard that sometimes people can be a bit hostile if they notice they’re being photographed, or may ask for money. I always try to be very respectful, quiet and discreet when I shoot, but I was wondering what your honest opinion is. If I’m travelling alone for a week, do you think a city like Rabat or Fez might be a better option for street photography, with people being a bit less confrontational? I’d really appreciate your thoughts. Thanks again for the great content.

author: @georgimihov2690
text: Old graves by/on the beach is actually a very common thing around the world - sunbathing and spending time on the beach is only a thing of the modern era. In previous centuries people had no use for beaches, as they are not arable land, so instead of creating cemeteries on good fertile land inland they would put them along a beach, or on the beach in some instances, to make use of otherwise useless space.

author: @gon20hig
text: Chellah (Shalla) and Hassan Mosque are two major historical sites in Rabat closely linked to Amazigh history. Chellah was originally inhabited by local Amazigh populations .the site later became the Roman city of Sala Colonia in the 1st century AD. During the Amazigh Almohad period in the 12th century, Chellah regained importance as a sacred area, before being developed into a fortified necropolis under the Amazigh Marinid dynasty in the 13th–14th centuries. Hassan Mosque and its minaret (Hassan Tower) were commissioned by Yaqub al-Mansur, an Amazigh Almohad caliph, around 1191–1197 CE, as part of an ambitious project to make Rabat an imperial capital, but construction stopped after his death in 1199 CE, leaving the tower unfinished and symbolic of Almohad architectural power.

I would urge anyone not to invest in Morroco as the people and system is corrupt and broken.

author: @DLPdiaries
text: Marrakech is so amazing! We stayed in a Riad in the Medina and did excursions to the Atlas mountains (blew our mind, so beautiful!), Essouira and a quad tour through the palmeraie. And of course wandered through the medina, had the best food and experiences in the gardens and djamaa El Fna etc. I would realy reccomend visiting. The Moroccans are SO welcoming and kind! You do need to keep in mind to haggle in the shops, and not fall for the monkey scams on the square or ' i'll show you the way' scams but other than that...such amazing people and such a beautiful culture!

text: Marrakesh is boring, the people working in the tourism industry are patronising and unfriendly, and you can get really ill eating the food. It was a massive disappointment and the most overrated destination I've been to in recent years. I live in Spain and there's more historic buildings in your average Spanish town than in the city of Marrakesh. The whole place is a big sell and a big con.  Avoid Marrakesh and probably Morocco. Africa is a big place and there are a lot more interesting and better value places to visit.

author: @truthsetyoufree104
text: The destinations you've explored don't capture the full essence of Morocco. A significant portion of the population grapples quietly with challenges like unemployment, inadequate education and healthcare, poverty, and mental health struggles. Additionally, a number of individuals, driven by desperation, risk their lives attempting to migrate to Europe. This is the stark reality that some influencers may overlook or choose not to share, but global awareness is growing, and the broader truths are increasingly hard to conceal.

author: @RightToLive3222
text:
Morroco is a corrupt country. The law and constitution is broken. 

Law and order only exists if bribes are paid. 

The court system is rigged where you always fall short of their demands. 

I was a landlord in Marrakesh. I tried to evict the tenant, the case is still active even after 4 years.

This was not a murder case. This was a case of evicting a tenant from my property. 

I would urge anyone not to invest in Morroco as the people and system is corrupt and broken.

Le Jardin Majorelle/YSL and Berber museum are wonderful - went the day after seeing Le Secret Jardin (their gift shop is amazing don't miss local artizens who work & sell there)  near the Riad we were staying at.  Highly recommend Medina Sky for dinner a very special place with amazing service and lastly go to the Atlas Mountains specifically  go to women's Collective Dar Zite - the best Argon Oil and mountain rose perfume.  Only hint while walking in Medina stay by the walls when the scooters go by.  They will run you over. Local and tourist alike. 
TIP:
To say "enjoy" in Moroccan Arabic, you would use the phrase "b-ssaha w-rraha"

author: @IkeOzurumba
text: i just got back from a vacation in Morocco (Marrakech and Essaouira), I cant stop thinking about it, I think Morocco might be my favorite place ive  traveled to so far. My biggest advice to people is to learn to simply ignore anyone who approaches you and tries to sell you something, offer you something, or assist you with something that you didnt ask them for. You simply have to. (and I do mean literally ignore them, dont pay them attention) Learn to do this and your time will be amazing! I really really loved this place. The people are so warm and inviting, the Medinas are so interesting and endlessly entertaining, the food, oh the food! Not everywhere is the best food (think tourist traps) but if you read up on some reviews you can really find some of the most amazing foods! I'm sitting here at home missing it all! Awesome video, helped me relive my awesome trip. Recently discovered you channel and love it.Using it to prep for new adventures! THanks!

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Kefta was what Kevin ate at nearly every meal! At Taj Moroccan Cuisine we had fried beldi eggs, wheat harira (which is what I think you may be calling a soup, but it's actually more like cream of wheat), moroccan mint tea, olive oil and jams, and homemade raib, and batbout bread. Chicken Pastilla was the pastry at Dar L’Hssira, and there also was couscous chicken. If you want to pinpoint where in the video you're most curious about regarding food, let me know (if I didn't reference it already). —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Donna, Knees and shoulders is more of a Western modesty ideal when you're heading into a church. Some Muslim women are covered head to toe. But at the same time, Marrakech and Fes and Chefchaouen are pretty tolerant of Westerners. We noticed a wide range of dress among locals and visitors alike. You don't need to wear headscarves anywhere, especially since you're not allowed in a mosque if you're not a Muslim (in Morocco). I felt my clothes were reasonably modest while also being comfortable for the heat. As long as you're not baring your midriff, showing off too much cleavage, or showing off your thighs, I think you're good. Bear in mind that some Moroccans wear more clothes because they're also trying not to tan too much/protect their skin versus exclusively for modesty purposes. We hope you have a terrific time in Morocco! —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi Laura, The culture in Morocco is more modest, so if you want to respect the local culture, you won't wear clothes that are too revealing. I'm wearing tank tops, and my skirts are close to knee length, but you could cover your arms with an airy blouse and wear loose long pants or long skirts. I wasn't going to go out and buy these items since we're full time travelers, but I tried to be as modest as possible given my limited wardrobe. Morocco is reasonably understanding of tourists and don't necessarily expect them to follow all the Muslim customs. Personally, I wouldn't wear crop tops or show off my stomach or my full back or wear a bathing suit top (although we saw all of these by tourists). Does this help at all? —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Angela, There are no required vaccinations that the Moroccan government checks to see if you have had. However, with the monkeys in the Sahara, it wouldn't be the worst thing to have a rabies vaccination (we do not have that vaccination). We made sure that we had Hepatitis A, B, tetanus, MMR (measles, mumps, and rubella), typhoid, and cholera before we traveled to Egypt. If you have any specific concerns, it's not a bad idea to check with a travel doctor who can give you recommendations. But specific to Morocco? Nothing is required for entry. —Judy
🎥 Why we got vaccinated in Rome before heading to Egypt: https://youtu.be/ZJG4MI76u58

text: ​@tkozikow hey there! So my best friend and I are heading to Marrakesh for my 30th birthday in July, and I was wondering if you wouldn't mind sharing a bit about your experience and places to go when we're there. We don't mind taking a day trip out of Marrakesh, so anything you can provide would be great!

author: @tkozikow
text:
​@Kaedehara_egiap we booked our excursions directly through our riad, and the highlight was a day trip to the Ourika Valley. We had a private driver who arranged for a guide in Setti Fadma to accompany us on a waterfall hike.   There are plenty of small bus/group tours, but I would highly recommend spending the extra money... It was around $175, including the tip, for two people. We stopped at a couple of Berber villages along the way and purchased some pottery and a rug for a fraction of the cost it would be in Marrakech. The guide was around $40 and well worth the money. If you decide to do this, be sure to have decent shoes that are OK for light hiking.

I suggest hiring a guide to explore the souks and key areas inside the medina. You might encounter some sales pitches along the way, but it was an interesting five hours and provided us with ideas for what to explore on our own. You can easily book either of these through a service like Viator, but if you're staying at a decent riad, they should be able to coordinate this for you.

Definitely explore the alleyways and small streets in the medina for the best food and a glimpse of the culture. Marakech is very tourist-friendly, with something like 80% of its revenue based on this, and we never felt unsafe.

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi @rosier3188, I want to be clear that I understand your question. I did book tours when I was in Morocco (but we are full-time travelers and that's the only time that worked for us—BUT we were there 40 days and weren't in a time crunch). We recommend ONLY ever booking your excursions online for the best experience. If you wait until you show up in Morocco and go to one of the vendors shops, you might be able to get a discount, BUT you will have a much lesser experience. We talk about it in our Sahara Desert video, and although the point we make is specifically for that type of excursion, it really applies to any type of excursion. On the street, they don't care what kind of experience you give them because there's a higher likelihood that (1) you won't leave a review; (2) people who book from those vendors don't typically even look at reviews; (3) they know that they are appealing to broad masses, so the entire experience tends to be more plain than what you book in advance. That's also because the vendors know that their reputation is on the line, which isn't necessarily the case for street vendors with shops. Note that it's illegal for them to work out the details with you on the street, so they need to hustle you into their kiosk to sort out the details.

If you are referring to our Sahara Desert tour, we would suggest you book it early online and talk with the vendor in advance so that you get exactly what you want. Here is our full series, which includes tons of details about our Sahara Desert tour and the vendor we used (not affiliated in any way and make no money from recommending them). If you need me to explain it better, please let me know! —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

@FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi again, @jmw-qt2ih! The food on the Sahara Desert tour will be the same food you get everywhere else in Morocco. Tagine will be everywhere and so will couscous. Couscous is its own meal served vegetarian with vegetables (large pieces of a few assorted vegetables like carrot, butternut squash, potato and chickpeas) or also including a choice of chicken, beef, or lamb. Tagine is basically meat with a few vegetables on top (butternut squash, carrots, and preserved lemons), which also can be vegetarian. Kevin ate a lot of kefta, which is basically beef meatballs in a tomato sauce. On the tour we also got a tomato/cucumber salad in most places. They often also had fruit as dessert, and bread was always served at meals. Our videos from Morocco show a fairly good representation of the food we ate throughout the 41 days we were in Morocco. Breakfasts would typically include yogurt, fresh fruit juice, tea, and sometimes pastries. 

Just to be a little clearer, we ate a breakfast the first day just as we were starting off at a coffee shop/grocery store for tourists and lunch was also at a local restaurant (the cost was not included in the excursion price). Dinner and breakfast the second morning was at our accommodations and included soup and was a bit fancier. Day 2 lunch was at a local restaurant (again you ordered off the menu and the cost was not included in the tour). Dinner was served family style in our desert accommodations and was very nice (chicken tagine with soup and fruit for dessert and bread). Breakfast at our desert accommodations included pastries, bread, yogurt. Lunch was at a restaurant and we again chose from a menu. 

Let me know if you have any other questions. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi @TranLe-vy5nz, People are friendly, but we suggest you don't take directions unless you are truly lost. Personally, we avoided some of the street food and stuck to restaurants because we didn't trust that our stomachs could handle it. Don't take pictures of people without their permission; I'd also plan to stick to the main streets of the Medina. It is easy to get lost and you might not want that to happen in the more remote passageways. We found Moroccans to be very kind and helpful, but crime can happen anywhere. We have many more suggestions in our Do's and Don'ts for Morocco, which is linked below in our full series from Marrakech.

We hope you have a great time! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
@priyankakamble12 Late February is considered shoulder season, which typically means that the prices are a bit lower than peak season. Marrakech and the Sahara Desert will have dry and sunny weather, although evenings and mornings will be cooler (lower 42°F (6°C)). Some places like Oukaimeden will potentially have snow, but the temperatures in Marrakech and Fes will be mild with daytime highs ranging from 63°F to 68°F (17°C to 20°C). Thanks for watching. Here is a link to our full series from our 41 days in Morocco. —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

text:
Hi @Judith-t8f, It is so much easier when you literally have no room. If you saw our episode in Marrakech when we first arrived, we made an effort to get rid of 12 pounds apiece of things in our suitcases. author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Dan, We understand that a lot of this is very cultural. I learned the hard way when I tried to take a photo of a butcher stall and the shop worker gave me the finger (and probably a few curse words). Some people in Morocco believe in the evil eye and are actually afraid of cameras. Some Muslims also have issues with "graven images" of themselves and having their picture taken is against their religious beliefs. Other people are just worried about someone copying their handiwork. Not everyone in Morocco feels this way, but we try to be respectful of those who do. 

But you are probably referring to places like the Yves Saint Laurent museum and Berber museum. We hate that, too. There were restrictions in certain places in Albania as well, and some places will allow you to take photos but not video. It's hard for us to go places that we can't share with our viewers, and we agree some of it feels petty. We've never encountered anything like Madrid where we were watched like hawks in a few of the museums there. And even when we weren't on YouTube, we still liked having photos as memories of our experiences, so thanks for relating! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi @dotted8- For some people in Morocco there's a fear of the evil eye, for others there's a fear of having their unique ideas stolen, and others have conservative religious beliefs that make them uncomfortable with photos. We try our best to respect the local customs wherever we visit, but you are entitled not to patronize a place that doesn't allow photos. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: I now am realizing that you were referring to the Berber museum and the Yves Saint Laurent museum. Yeah, not taking photos was supposed to keep the art there feeling "special," and perhaps "exclusive," but it's a huge bummer for us when photos are restricted. Sorry I didn't understand this better in my original response! I was mentally more focused on all the souks and people who didn't want their pictures taken. I'd almost forgotten about the issue with the museums (out of sight; out of mind—just one more reason that they SHOULD allow photos—so you can look back on those memories later!) —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Mike, We originally were going to spend time in Casablanca, but every local person told us not to bother because it's a business/commercial city without a lot of soul. We skipped Rabat, but primarily because we didn't want to do more travel. We were ready to move out of Marrakech by three weeks (instead of our usual month). We really enjoyed our Sahara Desert tour, which was a significant expense for the amount of time, but it was worth it. Fes was great, but also, by the end of nearly 18 days, we were ready to move on from there as well. We preferred Fes over Marrakech, but it was worth seeing them both. We took a day trip from Fes to Volubilis and Meknes, which we'll be sharing with you in our Fes video. 

You may have missed that we mention at the end when we talk about pricing that our Sahara Desert tour brought us to Fes, so we didn't have to get any transportation between cities per se. Thank you so much for watching and for your comment! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Thank you so much for watching and for letting us know about the Majorelle/YSL Museum combo you took. We'd heard good things about the YSL Museum, but since we couldn't take photos and share them with our viewers, it had slightly less appeal for us. And we heard mixed reviews about Jardin Majorelle because it had become a bit tired looking, but we can't say if that was actually the case. I didn't have much of the kefta until we got to Fes, where I ate significantly more of it, but Kevin's were always good. Fes had a bit more spice at times, which was a happy treat, so there were differences in flavors between cities. We considered seeing more of the country than we did, but ultimately were glad we were able to be immersed in Marrakech and Fes for longer. We did a day trip to Meknes and Volubilis as a day trip from Fes as well, that we'll be sharing in two weeks. Kevin changed it up with a few burgers (including camel burgers) and also pizza a couple of times in Fes. One nice thing about Fes was that our Riad's breakfast was really delicious and satisfying, so we only needed to go out for one meal per day, which slowed some of the tagine burnout for us! —Judy

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: I said in the video that I was overwhelmed with the noise, crowd and fellow tourists. I have a love-hate relationship with the market. And you have no right to say that we are dishonest in our video. We go out as early as possible to beat the crowd,  and heat and it is always quieter in the morning. It is your fault for going out late then complain if it’s crowded. You should know the culture of the places that you visit. We also gave tips in the video, which you would know, IF you watched and listened to be careful of scammers. We knew about that by watching other travel videos and reading blogs before going. Again, it was your fault for not preparing yourself mentally of all the possibilities. Tourists are there to learn their culture, not to change it. If you did not like your experience, you have no right to accuse us of being dishonest when we clearly mentioned everything. Next time, before you talk, you listen to everything we say and have better understanding. If you do not like the crowd, go in the mountains!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
It's Chano here answering your comment, I am from Spain and I grew up in a Christian family, in case you may think I am Muslim because I love travelling to Morocco and learning about their history and culture. 
There is not historic evidence about a church being located where the Koutoubia Mosque was built. But our Cathedral of Sevilla was built on top of a previous mosque, and the old minaret was transformed into the beautiful Giralda bell tower, which is considered the architectural sister of the Koutoubia Minaret. 
The Spanish Christians built many churches on top of mosques after the Reconquista of Al-Andalus, and the Arabs did the same before as well, building mosques on top of Roman temples and Visigothic churches. And the Romans did the same on top of other religions temples. It was very common to repurpose materials or sites from previous religious buildings.
If you try to spread misinformation in our content we will have to block you. We respect different religions and ideas, so please do not spam our comment section with hatred. Thanks.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Nassa.
Thanks a million for watching our video and for appreciating our work! 🙏🏽 We're very happy to read your positive feedback and very kind words. Comments like yours keep us motivated to continue working hard and to share more travel documentaries around the world in the future. 🎥💪🏽😊

Regarding the herbal infusion, we cannot remember the price, but it wasn't expensive at all, they said it was done with mint, but not sure if something else was included. We still have some crystals at home, we use them with very little quantity and put boiling water when we have very bad hay fever for example, it helps to open the nose and it feels refreshing in the throat too. We haven't drunk it anymore though, as it was too strong, and just inhaling the boiling water it is more than enough for us. 
The name they had on their sign is: Herboriste Des Amis, and they had the following phone number on the sign too: 024382076 (Morocco's international prefix: +212). Google Maps: https://share.google/KlQ2z5FO6dCXCQQfl
We advise you contact your doctor if you have some health issues before taking traditional medicines too. Hope that helps!

We wish you all the very best to you too, have a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️😊 Let us know if you have other questions in other videos, we are always happy to share information about travelling with our viewers. Best regards!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Thanks for watching. I think we have showed in the video that most local people were very friendly to us. Of course, like in all the big cities in the world, you need to have common sense. We have explained in the video about how to behave in the souks to avoid issues. Take the usual safety measures like the ones you would take visiting big cities like Rome, Paris or Madrid, just to mention some big cities we have visited in recent years, where you have to keep your belongings safe in crowded places. Do not believe scammers approaching you in the middle of the street. Do not stay alone late at night in areas where there are not many people. In our whole trip in Morocco we felt safe, you can watch the rest of our travel series to have a broader idea. We caught public buses (even at night), the train, we stayed after sunset and woke up for sunrise. Not a single issue. We met some wonderful people who just wanted to chat on the streets too. If someone suddenly offers you to bring you somewhere or to sell you something, in any country in the world, just don't trust them. If they are just curious and friendly, be open to a nice conversation. 🙂

text: Thanks for watching! We walked everywhere in Marrakech, but we visited the Jardin Majorelle on our last day in the city at the end of the whole Morocco trip. We were coming back to Marrakech from Casablanca by train, so we stayed one last night in a different hotel called Hotel Racine, which is closer to the train station and to the Jardin Majorelle, so we walked from there instead. From the Riad Dreams you would need to walk 30 minutes to the Jardin Majorelle, so we would advise to get a taxi (always agree the price before entering the vehicle) if you stay there and want to visit the gardens. Have a lovely trip!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Thank you for watching our video!

We didn´t exchange money in Morocco, we have a Revolut card that allow us to withdraw money for free in other countries up to 200€ per month, and then after that we pay only 1€ commission for every withdrawal. But you have to find the proper ATMs or Banks in every country to avoid paying commissions there. In Morocco, we only used the Al Barid Bank ATMs and we never paid commission with them. I would advise you to find them in Google Maps in every city in advance to save their location, there are not that many and better be prepared with enough cash, but if you are only visiting Marrakech, you have an Al Barid Bank ATM at the airport in the departures area (walk to the right side immediately before exiting out the airport). Then you have 2 or 3 more Al Barid Bank ATMs in the Jemma el-Fnaa square just outside the Post Office building. Sometimes we could pay in Restaurants and Hotels directly with our Revolut debit card. They always have great currency exchange.

We often show dishes with meat as you can see in our video, but we remember that in almost every restaurant that we show in this video, they had vegetarian dishes as well, you can find easily falafel, vegetarian couscous or vegetarian tajine. The tacos can be found without meat as well and they often have soups and salads done with only vegetables. So I think Morocco is a good country for a vegetarian to try local cuisine as well. And don´t forget their desserts! ☺️

Hope our reply was useful. Wish you a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️

So, ladies can wear whatever they want in public, in fact, sometimes we saw some local young women showing their legs with modern outfits, and many tourists were wearing short dresses as well. But at the same time, Morocco is a muslim country and they have certain cultural traditions and I preferred to respect that during our visit. I had heard some women receiving bad comments for dressing too sexy while visiting Morocco, but I only received smiles & nice comments about my dresses and the locals were often happy to see me with certain clothes. My husband helped me to choose some dresses before we visited, because he had already been there and he also knew the best colours for matching with the architecture 😅
In December, it may be cold early in the morning and at night too, so be careful with that, specially in the north of the country and in the desert. The Moroccan sun is very strong during the day, so traditional long clothing is actually the best for the desert for example. 
Hope my answer helped you a little bit!
All the best and wish you have a wonderful trip there!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Hello there Tom! There are indeed scams but for us they are very obvious. Also it was a great help that we watched a lot of videos before going there so we already knew what to avoid. For example, a random man who says that the way is closed then he will guide us to the correct way. Then he will ask money when you will arrive to the place. Do not believe them and just say no thank you. A lot of the merchants will be very friendly to you too and ask where are you from to start a conversation. We just answered them politely but we kept walking to where we were headed. If you know are lost for example in the souks because it’s like a labyrinth, just ask a merchant and probably avoid asking a random guy in the street. We were fine. We avoided “rough” areas. We used common sense. They are not going to hurt you. Some of them are just trying to earn money. Just ignore or say thank you. We felt safe in general. A lot of them are very friendly too. We met a family who just wanted to speak and practice their English. We are still in contact with them and invited us to visit their town.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
About the second question, Sardinia and Morocco are totally different. It depends on what you want to experience.

Sardinia is for beautiful Mediterranean coasts, coastal towns, chill life, amazing well kept nature, friendly locals, good food.

Morocco if you want to experience North African culture, more lively chaotic but unique culture, good food, mix of African and European architecture. And by the way, Moroccan architecture is impeccable and incomparable. They have one of the best designs I’ve ever seen. As a matter of fact, I want a touch of Moroccan design in my future house. Haha!! 

So if you decide which ones you want to experience, choose one. If you have more questions, feel free to ask us. We have Sardinia videos too.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Also, be careful of other videos describing the scams in Morocco. We found them too exaggerated. We’re not saying that scamming doesn’t exist but sometimes they were too much. And I think it is becoming more and more famous through social media so they are more careful now too.

author: @duffgordon9005
text: Tangier was THE PICK of ATCO RECORDS (CREME) for that great label (on hiatus) to be 'reborn' . They hired Andy Johns as Producer - did the MTV films.. put a lot of $$ into them.and promotion. They toured with Cinderalla , 38 Special-- If you go to Amazon and put in their LP's , you will read, from HARD CORE ROCKERS.. 5 star after 5 star, and even Anger- "how come I never knew about these guys"!!  The problem imo was 3-fold. 1) It was the end of the glammish rockers, and the opposite came flooding in- KURT COBAIN and the like 2)  The key to the staying power is "a HIT"  if not number having a number one- All their soings are "hittable" but it just did not happen. I always say, play ALL three LP's and chances are you will not sigh and "skip songs here and there. " Doug Gordon, started playing guitar at 16, and got signed at 28- supplying dozens of songs, and was featured in Guitar PLayer. He always said, I am either going to make it, and continue on, or "that's that" It's a testament that their music has not aged.I love the Remastering of 4-winds, . my suprise is not a single Movie, Commercial ever got picked out say "FEVER for GOLD " on one of these many mining shows..

author: @nathanlansford1882
text:
Plenty of people wanted it though, by that point. They were sick of hundreds of pop-glam metal bands doing the same types of songs with the same types of lyrics all the time. 

From 1988 to '91, that was increasingly the case. And it wasn't only the grunge fans that felt that way about it--so did the underground speed/thrash, death/grindcore & black metal fans (& bands).

author: @rotadymaify

Of course I like a lot of styles of music but Hair metal or hard rock from late 70’s to early 90’s is a part of a lot of peoples best times of their lives. There was never a better time to be alive.

author: @ArbitusRM
text:
You know I don't think they realise that by shoving their prices through the roof during an event like the FIFA is going on ends up in the long run putting a taint for most people booking with them again.
I live in Niagara Falls, Ontario and obvious tour town and I have friends that work in hotels and event locations around the city and area.
No hotel here, at least in the past, automatically shoves stupid prices in place because there's going to be an event on a particular weekend.
Yes they'll raise the base price on that weekend by 20%-30% because of an event but not 300% to 500% before an event as most set prices for hotels here based on an "on demand" system.  
This day and age, unless the hotel is an independent privately owned hotel they are most likely owned or part of a hotel network and have access to that hotel's marketing system that gives them enough data so they know just how much is booked, is available and what others in the system are paying and/or prescribe to one of the local marketing companies that routinely keep track of the prices themselves.
So here, for the most part, set their prices higher as the demand goes up because they know it's better to have a full hotel where you are getting, on average, more than they would get a regular night then it is to set prices so high that while they might sell all the rooms for one night on the event night(s) they've ended up blowing off customers in the process who would have paid a lower rate but booked 2, 3, 4+ nights instead of just 1 night.
A 120 room hotel will make a lot more money selling out rooms at moderately higher rates for 3+ nights booked across the event day than the same hotel selling out for 1 event night at an unfathomable rate for that night.
What's better?  
Renting a room to an international or even a national tourists for $900 on the night of the event?
Or renting to an international or again even a national tourist for 3 or more nights across the event date for say $450 a night?

To me I would avoid, in the future, any hotel/hotel chain that's going to screw people over like that!!

author: @manub.3847
text:
Regarding the 2022 World Cup, I had read that hotel and accommodation bookings were impossible without a World Cup ticket number; furthermore, even six months prior to the event, the accommodation booking portal had not yet opened, as not all accommodations were ready.
Therefore, to compare this situation with bookings for the USA—and to hope that bookings will start rolling in now—is idiotic.
If one compares this booking pattern with the 2006 World Cup in Germany, it becomes apparent that accommodations in the host cities—and in some cases even nearby campsites—were already fully booked six months prior to the tournament.

We were let out at the visitor's center and informed that they were filming a movie in the historic area.  We could go in anyway, if we stayed out of the cameras' way.  Mom and I said, let's go.  We found them filming exterior shots along one street, and located a hamburger stand behind the camera set up.  We had lunch watching them film antique cars and a tram over and over,  talked to a few extras in costume, and took pictures of the cameras and a wagon full of palm trees.  It was great.  When we got back to the visitor's center, we discovered no one else had left the building, scared off by the idea of the camera crew.  A vacation is what you make of it.

Las Ramblas, Barcelona lol in 2007 we were strolling down the sidewalk in the evening (with a 8 year old and a 15 year old) and my husband and I looked up to see a few transvestites. We immediately turned around.

But I found the subway to be more of a pick pocket hazard. I had my 8 year old by the hand as we stepped on. Immediately 2 men stepped between us. I got loud and didnt let go of my son. I also had my money in a pouch under my clothing. The two men immediately got off.

The worst pick pocket experience I had tho was in Rome near the Colosseum. We decided to take a short cut on our way back to our hotel. I was immediately mobbed by a bunch of kids one of whom had a square of cardboard which she pushed up against my chest. The idea is that while she got my attention, one of the other kids would be underneath picking my pocket. Too bad for them my money was in a pouch they didnt have access too.

I’ve been to visit the Pyramids. It was a 4 day stop as a part of our honeymoon. Luckily we had booked through travel agency so we had guides. We werent bothered by people trying to seperate us from our money. The Pyramids are a site to see.

Trevi Fountain is very crowded (thus the high rate of pick pockets). It’s very beautiful.

author: @johncraven9719
text:
The subject seems a non-starter.

One might think that living or realising a dream would always give pleasure to someone. But buying a product without really asking the right questions is almost bound to lead to worries, anger, disappointment.
BTW one of life's principles is "disappointment is the inevitable result of expecting too much". Many would book a cheap hôtel in order to increase their holiday spending budget or because of their youthful enthusiasm, but would be stupid to complain about the lack of comfort or quiet!
Those who travel while constantly comparing things to "how it was back home" are also bound to become narrow-minded and dissatisfied. To voyage with a sense of curiosity and discovery is a better mindset.

Then again do you want sandy beaches, mountain lakes, hiking, total relaxing, a hired car, being part of a tourist guided group, alone, with someone special? Have you got travel within the country sorted out? Do you know what currency is used (e.g. not every member country of the European Union uses the Euro as currency!)

author: @missusd1130
text: To avoid in Wisconsin: My home state: Events: The WI state fair, for much of the reasons stated here, also drinking events in Madison, for CRIME reasons, including being given knockout and rape drugs, esp. during Freak fest and the Mifflin street block party, and The Hodag Festival which turns out to be mostly a hidden Stugris drug, drunk, prostitute and hidden theft fest on hidden steroids.  If you want family friendly, county fairs are the way to go.  If you need something "more exciting", I suggest the Dells preferably out of the most active time--less expsneisve, less crowded, and way easier to schedule in the "off season" between mid August-mid June.  Yeah, avoid July visits.  Milwaukee should be avoided entirely unless you want to see things like the Betty Brin and other museums outside of crowded times.  Stay out of Milwaukee at night!  Don't park except in paid places, You car MIGHT be watched in those areas.  Most other places in WI are great including Door County, but look for a mosquito or tick report.  You'll thank me.  Biting flies can get guaranteed when those are active and some don't give a rip about DEET.  We've had a first hand experience of DEET failing to even affect ticks and biting flies, mine included a cases of Babiosis.  My recommendation, Visit in August through November to take advantage of the lovely foliage and harvest fest which can be quite family friendly and still have the brewskis you're wanting.  Easily Spring in great too, the bugs are still dead and the worst thing you'll experience is mud.  The camping is best in May and early June but in some places the ticks are out already.  August if ideal drying out and the sicks start to die out.  In SD where I live now stay away from Crazy Horse Monument.  It's not done and insanely expensive.  THey're right about Mt Rushmore, the parking is insanely expensive and don't visit on a cloudy day!  Buy a start park pass the SD state parks are all great, not just Custer and really worth visiting and has less ticks and mosquitoes than WI except during July  Outside of July and January/February and with a careful following of weather reports (because in May we get summer one day and winter the next LOL!).  If you're a cheapie, Spearfish anyone is FREE to explore!  And the city of Spearfish is a tourist paradise with not too far Rapids City having a lot of great sights, places to visit and even hiking areas that are great views and not hard to manage.

author: @rickymcc8624
text:
For sure just about anywhere can feel like "hell on Earth" if you choose to go unprepared, in high season, without doing proper research first and managing your expectations.

I have seen and enjoyed many of these, but season and time of day matters hugely.

IMO it's best to avoid anywhere when a massive cruise ship docks and a sunami of tourists wash inland.

So my top tips.
1) Do lots of research before travelling. Read Trip advisor reviews and if possible advice from friends who might have already been to place X.

2) Set realistic expectations, based on your research.

3) Ideally travel in the 'quieter' season. Get up and out at first light 5, 6 or 7am to have a real chance of missing the crowds.

author: @mtnsidhe
text:
Having been to a number of these places, I don't agree with all of these assessments. For instance, travelers should always expect and protect themselves from pickpockets, especially in Europe.

Pisa should be a 2-hour stop between other places, and do climb to the top if you can. Avoid the toilets in the restaurants, they are literally a white-tiled hole in the floor.

Venice is just awful. Not only crowded, over priced, touristy and smelly, but also hot, muggy, and mosquito-infested. There are no screens on the windows and no AC, so sleeping at night requires open windows, and fat, blood-filled mosquitos in your room come morning. If you do go, be sure to see St. Mark's Square and Cathedral and feed the pigeons for a photo.


Versailles should be on this list. Long lines, huge entrance fee, shuffling through one enormous cube-shaped room after another, all filled with gilt-framed paintings and ostentatious rococco furniture and priceless gewgaws, all behind ropes, over and over and over until they all blur together. Lots of walking and standing in return for feeling simultanelously overwhelmed and underwhelmed, plus tired and bored. There is a separate tour for the gardens, which may have been better, but still, probably lots of walking, only in the sun.

author: @hraphael101
text:
Avantage !

La règle de l’avantage au football (Loi 5) permet à l’arbitre de ne pas interrompre le jeu après une faute si cela désavantage l’équipe victime. Le jeu se poursuit alors lorsqu’une action prometteuse est en cours. L’arbitre le signale en tendant les deux bras vers l’avant pour indiquer que l’équipe conserve le bénéfice de l’action.

C’est ainsi au football!

Lors du match Sénégal vs Maroc 2025, après qu’un penalty a été accordé à la 97e minute en faveur du Maroc, les joueurs sénégalais — à l’exception de leur capitaine, Sadio Mané — ont quitté le terrain pour protester. Voilà la faute. À ce moment-là, la majorité d’entre eux a choisi de déclarer forfait plutôt que de risquer une défaite jugée injuste.

Les Marocains (l'équipe victime) auraient certes remporté le match s'ils avaient accepté le forfait à ce moment-là, mais ils ne voulaient pas gagner de cette manière. Une victoire par forfait n’aurait pas eu la même valeur qu’une « vraie » victoire.

C’est pourquoi, lorsque Sadio Mané a rappelé ses coéquipiers en affirmant qu’il valait mieux perdre « comme des hommes » que de déclarer forfait et couvrir le Sénégal de honte, l’ensemble des Marocains — joueurs, entraîneurs, supporters, et même le roi — s’en sont réjouis. À la reprise du match, le penalty devait être joué et constituer la dernière action.

La probabilité d’une victoire marocaine était alors extrêmement élevée. La victoire marocaine était, à toute fin pratique, dans la poche. C'est la raison pour laquelle, les Marocains ont choisi, sans y être contraints, d’ignorer le fait que plusieurs joueurs sénégalais avaient quitté le terrain pendant près de 15 minutes et ont décidé de poursuivre le match. Ils ont choisi de jouer l’avantage, parce qu'ils étaient convaincus à ce moment là que le destin les avait choisis.

Coup de théâtre :  Díaz, la star marocaine, manque complètement sa Panenka. Le score reste de 0-0 à la fin du temps réglementaire. Le pari marocain est perdu.

Lorsque le Sénégal leur avait offert le match, ils auraient pu — et peut-être dû — l’accepter. Mais ce qu’ils recherchaient, c’était le règne, la puissance et la gloire… la vraie gloire.

Désormais, il est trop tard pour revenir en arrière. Le match se poursuit, et, en prolongation, les Sénégalais inscrivent un superbe but qui leur offre la victoire.

Ainsi, quitter le terrain peut être perçu comme une faute, mais les Marocains ont choisi de jouer l’avantage.

Et cette fois, cela leur a coûté cher.

Victoire bien méritée du Sénégal. 

 

Note: Je vous fais grâce des vols de serviettes, des empoisonnements alimentaires et du manque de sécurité lors de leurs déplacements sur le territoire marocain.

author: @soba_entertainment
text:
Senegal won, and here are some valid points:
1. They returned to the game in record time.
2. If it was indeed a forfeit, why didn't the referee blow the final whistle then, and why didn't Morocco report the misconduct in real-time?
3. Morocco agreed to continue playing and missed their penalty; had they scored, they would likely be the legitimate AFCON winners.
4. If CAF can reverse Senegal's win for "leaving the pitch," then Morocco's actions in 1976 should also be reconsidered, despite them winning the cup.
5. This appears to be a case of bias; Morocco is bitter because they lost a game that was seemingly rigged in their favor.
6. That was arguably the most contentious final ever, with the referee favoring Morocco 90% of the game.

My conclusion: if Senegal's trophy is to be revoked, then Morocco's 1976 trophy, Argentina's 1986 trophy (due to Maradona's handball), and other past unfair decisions should also be revisited for consistency.

author: @Andalus4Ever
text:
Let’s look at it from a regulatory standpoint, because several key concepts are being misunderstood.

First: the argument that “the referee’s decision is final” is being misapplied.

Yes, under Law 5, the referee’s decisions during the match are final in terms of on-field play.

But that does not mean the result is immune from post-match disciplinary review.

That’s a completely separate layer of governance.

Now, the core issue here is straightforward: a team leaving the field of play without the referee’s permission constitutes a regulatory breach.

And the rules on that are not ambiguous:

- Leaving the field without authorization is considered a serious violation

- It can lead to a forfeit, regardless of what happens afterward

So the argument that “they didn’t leave the stadium, only the pitch” is irrelevant.

The obligation is to participate in the match on the field of play, not merely to remain somewhere inside the venue.

Now, here’s the key misunderstanding:

“The game restarted, so everything before that is erased.”

That is simply incorrect.

Football governance clearly distinguishes between:

- The sporting outcome on the field, and

- The regulatory outcome after review

A referee allowing play to continue does not nullify a prior breach of the rules.

Disciplinary bodies exist precisely to address situations where infractions occurred, regardless of how the match was managed in real time.

Let me put it in a simple analogy:

Imagine a marathon runner who takes a shortcut off the official route, then re-enters the race and crosses the finish line first. At the moment, officials might not stop them. The race “finishes.”

But once the violation is reviewed, the result is corrected — because the rules of the competition were broken.

The same principle applies here.

Finally, claims of “disgrace,” “illegality,” or “backroom decisions” are speculative and not substantiated by the regulatory framework. These decisions are made within established procedures, and they remain subject to appeal — including at CAS.

So the bottom line is this:

Emotions about fairness are understandable.
But they do not override the rules.

And the rules are clear:
if a team leaves the field of play without authorization, it exposes itself to disciplinary sanctions — including forfeiture — even if the match was later resumed.

That’s not opinion.
That’s the regulatory framework.

Dima Maghrib ❤️🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦❤

author: @oulaich
text:
The truth about the AFCON 2025 final is simple: Football is governed by laws, not by who puts more pressure on the referee. When the Senegalese team decided to walk off the pitch for nearly 15 minutes in the 83rd minute, they didn’t just protest a call—they officially forfeited the match under CAF Regulations.
​According to Articles 82 and 84 of the tournament rules, any team that refuses to play or leaves the ground before the end of the match is considered the loser by a 3-0 scoreline. It is also important to recognize the impossible position the referee was put in. Faced with a complete walk-off and massive pressure from his superiors to avoid a total riot in the stands or further chaos on the field, he was forced to restart a game that legally should have ended right then and there.
​By forcing a restart on their own terms after such a long delay, Senegal disrupted the spirit of the final and ruined the celebration for millions. You cannot pause a continental final for 15 minutes and then expect the result to stand. Morocco stayed on the pitch, respected the referee, and followed the professional code of conduct. No matter what happened in extra time, the match was legally over the moment they walked toward the tunnel. Rules are rules, and in Rabat, the rule of law prevailed over the chaos.

author: @mightGalaxyBlackhole
text:
Embarrassing. Morocco should be ashamed of pursuing this title after they lost bitterly and justly to Senegal. 

The referee holds the ultimate authority and must be respected if you do not want to lose the match.

In this case, the match was restarted and eventually completed, despite the earlier disruption. Yes, the Senegalese team may have left the pitch at some point, but they returned and finished the game. That is what matters.

The referee is, in effect, the supreme authority on the field, the final arbiter. Once the match was concluded under his authority, that result should stand. CAF’s appeal committee should recognize this and refrain from overturning what was decided on the pitch. All the rules should consider that the match was completed on the pitch. 

Simply put, this trophy belongs to Senegal because the game was played to completion. We must avoid technicalities in our beautiful game.

author: @Slixis_3
text:
“IF A TEAM LEAVES THE FIELD” refers to the situation where the team, as a collective entity, leaves the field of play. In the present case, however, several players remained on the pitch. At most, this was only a partial departure by some players. Therefore, the automatic application of the article is not justified. It is a misreading of the text, arbitrary and unfair. THIS IS NOT LAW.
Had the referee definitively stopped the match, invoking Article 82 to award the championship title to one of the teams would have been legitimate. But the match continued. The victory was officially validated on paper. Law 5 of the International Football Association Board (IFAB), a fundamental principle of football law, states that the referee’s decisions regarding facts connected with play are final and irreversible. That simply settles the matter. Senegal retains the title. Any other decision would be contrary to morality.

author: @kisuke_1815
text:
To my dear European friends, have you forgotten the examples I will mention below that took place in your own continent, or do you not accept that African institutions can achieve justice? Do you expect Africa to remain associated with widespread mismanagement? It is time to move beyond this sense of superiority.

Football has already shown many times that matches and decisions don’t simply end with the final whistle—rules and justice can still be applied afterward, as seen in cases involving clubs like Inter Milan and Juventus. The 2006 Calciopoli scandal demonstrated that titles can be stripped and reassigned after proven violations, proving that outcomes can be corrected when wrongdoing is established.

A famous example is the 1991 match between AC Milan and Olympique de Marseille, where after a short interruption due to floodlights, Milan refused to resume play. As a result, Marseille was awarded the win and Milan faced sanctions, including exclusion from the competition.
This clearly shows that any team that refuses to continue or leaves the pitch must face the consequences—otherwise, it would open the door to chaos in football.

text:
@MarwaneKebir L'indépendance et la souveraineté se mesurent justement à la capacité d'un État à diversifier ses alliances (États-Unis, Chine, Turquie, Europe) pour défendre ses intérêts nationaux. Utiliser des slogans comme 'forces impérialistes' occulte la réalité d'un monde multipolaire où le Maroc joue sa propre carte pour sa stabilité.

On peut tout à fait débattre des défis sociaux internes et des politiques d'urbanisme, qui sont des sujets légitimes. Mais cela ne change rien à la réalité factuelle de la percée diplomatique du Maroc à l'international. Mélanger les problèmes de gestion locale et la stratégie géopolitique est un raccourci qui n'aide pas à comprendre la complexité du monde actuel.

author: @Wahid-Atlas
text:
@BHA231 Mélanger des griefs idéologiques et des attaques sur l'identité marocaine ne change pas la réalité du terrain.

Diplomatie : Le plan d’autonomie est aujourd’hui la seule option qualifiée de « sérieuse et crédible » par les dernières résolutions de l'ONU. Le monde privilégie le pragmatisme face à un référendum techniquement inapplicable depuis 30 ans.

Identité : La composante hébraïque est inscrite dans la Constitution marocaine ; ce n'est pas une « option stratégique » récente, mais une réalité millénaire.

Souveraineté : Elle s'exprime par le développement et l'adhésion des populations locales, pas par le maintien de conflits gelés.

Le vent tourne car la stabilité régionale exige des solutions définitives plutôt que des rhétoriques d'un autre temps.
